journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Cardiology. Heart & Vasculature

title-group.article-title: Dual-chamber pacing for vasovagal syncope: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials

contrib-group.aff: aCardiologic Unit, Specialistic Medicine Department, Guglielmo da Saliceto Hospital, Piacenza, Italy
bNational Institute for Cardiovascular Research (INRC), Via Irnerio 48, 40126 Bologna, Italy
cDepartment of Medical and Surgical Sciences for Children and Adults, University of Modena and Reggio Emilia, Via del Pozzo 71, 41124 Modena, Italy
dOncology and Hematology Department, Azienda Ospedaliero-Universitaria Policlinico Di Modena 41125 Modena, Italy

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Vasovagal syncope (VVS) is the most common cause of transient loss of consciousness and may be recurrent and disabling. Dual-chamber cardiac pacing has been proposed for patients with cardioinhibitory VVS, but randomized trials have yielded inconsistent results, influenced by patient selection and trial blinding.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials evaluating dual-chamber pacing in patients with recurrent cardioinhibitory VVS. PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Library were searched from inception to May 2025. Risk ratios (RRs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were pooled using fixed- or random-effects models as appropriate. Prespecified subgroup analyses were performed according to trial blinding status and selection for documented asystolic VVS.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Six randomized controlled trials including 429 patients were identified, evaluating rate-drop response, closed-loop stimulation, or DDI pacing. Overall, active pacing significantly reduced syncope recurrence compared with control (RR 0.47; 95% CI 0.27–0.82). The treatment effect was larger and statistically significant in open-label trials (RR 0.22; 95% CI 0.11–0.46), whereas blinded trials showed a non-significant trend toward benefit (RR 0.59; 95% CI 0.32–1.08). In patients with documented asystolic VVS, pacing significantly reduced recurrent syncope (RR 0.35; 95% CI 0.22–0.55), while no significant benefit was observed in patients not selected for asystolic episodes.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Dual-chamber pacing was associated with a lower incidence of syncope recurrence across randomized trials in patients with cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope. However, blinded studies did not demonstrate a statistically significant benefit, and the observed effect appears to be largely influenced by open-label trial designs.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Syncope is defined as a transient loss of consciousness characterized by rapid onset, short duration, and spontaneous recovery. Its estimated incidence is 6.2 per 1000 patients per year, with a cumulative lifetime incidence of approximately 35%. Vasovagal syncope (VVS) is the most common type, accounting for 21.2% of all syncopal events [1], [2].

The pathophysiology of VVS involves vasodilation—primarily of the splanchnic and lower limb vessels—leading to exaggerated hypotension and bradycardia or asystole. Clinically, VVS is often preceded by prodromes such as sweating, nausea, or pallor. When prodromes are present, patients can perform counter-pressure maneuvers to reduce recurrence; however, in some cases prodromes are absent or of short duration, increasing anxiety and negatively impacting quality of life.

VVS can be classified according to the predominant hemodynamic response: vasodepressive, cardioinhibitory, or mixed. The 2021 European Society of Cardiology (ESC) guidelines on cardiac pacing recommend pacemaker implantation for the prevention of recurrent cardioinhibitory reflex syncope [3]. Specifically, pacing is indicated (Class I) in patients over 40 years of age with severe, unpredictable, and recurrent syncope—particularly in those with absent or short-lasting prodromes—and with documented cardioinhibitory responses, including carotid sinus syndrome, tilt-induced asystole, or asystole >3 s with syncope or >6 s without syncope recorded by implantable loop recorder.

Earlier guidelines, including the 2017 ACC/AHA and 2018 ESC syncope guidelines, recommended pacemaker implantation with Class IIb indication in patients with recurrent, severe VVS and asystolic tilt-test responses [4], [5]. The transition to a Class I recommendation in the ESC 2021 guidelines was largely driven by evidence from the BioSync CLS trial, which demonstrated that closed-loop stimulation (CLS) pacing in patients over 40 years with recurrent VVS significantly reduced syncope recurrence compared to pacemaker implantation in OFF mode [6].

Over the past two decades, multiple randomized clinical trials have evaluated the effect of pacemaker therapy on VVS recurrence, yielding conflicting results. The present meta-analysis aims to synthesize this evidence to clarify the role of dual-chamber pacing in patients with recurrent cardioinhibitory VVS and to provide context for current guideline recommendations.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines were adhered to in the conducting of this systematic review and meta-analysis. The systematic review protocol was published a priori on PROSPERO. The registration number is CRD420251157810 [7].

sec.title: Data sources and searches
sec.p: A systematic literature search was conducted in PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Library databases from their inception to May 2025. The search strategy combined relevant keywords and Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) related to pacemaker implantation and cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope (e.g., “cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope; pacing cardiac and syncope”).

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: Studies were eligible for inclusion if they evaluated the efficacy or outcomes of pacemaker implantation in patients with cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope, as confirmed by either tilt-table testing or implantable loop recorder (ILR) findings. Eligible study designs included only randomized controlled trials. Prospective cohort studies, retrospective analyses, case reports, reviews, conference abstracts, and studies without extractable outcome data were excluded.

sec.title: Data extraction and quality assessment
sec.p: Two independent reviewers (F.S., G.P.) screened the titles and abstracts of all identified articles to assess their eligibility. The full texts of potentially relevant studies were then independently reviewed by the same two reviewers according to predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Any discrepancies between reviewers were resolved through discussion or consultation with a third reviewer (L.R). For each included study, the following information was recorded: first author, year of publication, study design, sample size, patient characteristics, diagnostic criteria for cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope (tilt-table testing or implantable loop recorder findings), pacemaker type and implantation protocol, follow-up duration, and primary outcomes (e.g., recurrence of syncope).

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: The primary outcome was the number of patients experiencing at least one recurrence of syncope during follow-up, as reported in each randomized controlled trial.

sec.title: Subgroup analyses
sec.p:
Prespecified subgroup analyses were performed to explore potential sources of heterogeneity and effect modifiers:•
Blinding status: outcomes were compared between blinded (single- or double-blind) versus open-label trials to assess potential placebo or nocebo effects.•
Asystolic VVS selection: outcomes were compared between patients with documented asystolic vasovagal syncope (ILR-documented asystole ≥ 3 s with syncope or ≥ 6 s without syncope, or tilt-induced asystole ≥ 3 s) versus patients not selected for asystolic episodes.

sec.title: Risk of bias in individual studies
sec.p: The methodological quality of the included RCTs was independently assessed by two reviewers using the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2 (RoB 2) tool, covering the following domains: randomization process, deviations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, outcome measurement, and selective reporting. Discrepancies were resolved by consensus. The table reporting the RoB 2 risk of bias assessment is provided in the Supplementary Data.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical analysis and the generation of forest plots were performed using RevMan 5.4 (Cochrane Collaboration, London, UK). Dichotomous outcomes were summarized as risk ratios (RRs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Heterogeneity was assessed using the Chi-square test and quantified with the I2 statistic. A random-effects model was applied in cases of substantial heterogeneity (I2 > 50%), while a fixed-effect model was used otherwise. Subgroup analyses were performed, and the p for interaction was calculated to assess differences between subgroups. To further explore sources of heterogeneity and the influence of individual studies on the pooled effect estimate, a Baujat plot was generated. Additionally, sensitivity analyses were conducted in R using restricted maximum likelihood estimation (REML) to assess robustness to different between-study variance estimators.

sec.title: Literature search
sec.p:
The search retrieved 184 studies results of which 44 duplicate records were manually removed. Following title and abstract review, 118 were excluded. Of the remaining 22 full text articles, 16 were excluded for the specified reasons illustrated in Fig. 1. The remaining 6 studies were included.Fig. 1
PRISMA Flow Diagram. Study Selection Process. Flow diagram illustrating the identification, screening, eligibility assessment, and inclusion of randomized controlled trials in the systematic review and meta-analysis according to PRISMA guidelines. Numbers of records identified, duplicates removed, full-text articles assessed, and studies included in the quantitative synthesis are displayed. Abbreviations: PRISMA = Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses; RCT = randomized controlled trial.

sec.title: Characteristics of the included studies
sec.p:
A total of 429 patients with suspected recurrent cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope were evaluated across 6 prospective randomized trials [Table 1]. The risk of bias assessment using the RoB 2 tool indicated that two trials (VPS I and Sutton) were overall at high risk of bias, mainly due to lack of blinding and open-label design, whereas the remaining four trials were judged at low risk of bias (Supplementary Fig. 1). Across the included studies, randomized patients had a history of recurrent syncope with either a mixed or asystolic response to tilt testing, or documentation of an asystolic pause on implantable loop recorder (ILR). In most of the active treatment arms, pacemaker therapy was delivered predominantly in the rate-drop response mode, while only one study employed closed-loop stimulation. There was some heterogeneity in the control groups: in two studies patients did not undergo pacemaker implantation, in two studies patients received a pacemaker programmed to OOO (inactive) mode, and in the remaining two studies patients received a pacemaker programmed to sensing-only ODO mode. Follow-up ranged from 6 to 44 months across the trials. Mean patient age varied between 43 and 63 years.Table 1
Characteristics and Outcomes of Randomized Controlled Trials Included in the Meta-AnalysisExplanation. Summary of six randomized controlled trials evaluating dual-chamber pacing (closed-loop stimulation [CLS], rate-drop response [RDR], or DDI) for recurrent cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope. For each study, details on study design, active and control interventions, number of patients, mean follow-up, mean age, and number of patients experiencing recurrent syncope are provided. Abbreviations used in the table are explained in the footnote. This table allows comparison across trials and provides context for the pooled effect estimates presented in the meta-analysis.
StudyStudy designActive groupControl GroupN. pt.FU (Month)Age (Mean)Syncopal recurrence
S.J.Connolly et al. (1999)randomized, not-blindDDD PM with RDRNot PM5415 (max)43 ± 186/27pt vs 19/27pt; p < 0.01
Sutton et al. (2000)randomized, not-blindDDI PM (LR 80 bpm)Not PM4244,4 ± 26,4 (Mean)601pt vs 14pt; p < 0.01
S.J.Connolly et al.(2003)randomized, double blindDDD PM with RDRPM ODO1006 (max)4916pt vs 22pt
Raviele et al.(2004)randomized, double blindDDD PM with RDRPM off2923,5 [10], [11], [12], [3](Median)53 ± 168pt vs 5pt
M. Brignole et al.(2012)randomized, double blindDDD PM with RDRPM ODO7724 (max)638pt vs 19pt
M.Brignole et al.(2020)randomized, double blindDDD PM with CLSPM off12711,2 [2.5–22.1](Median)62 ± 1210pt vs 34pt; p < 0.01
Abbreviations (example): CLS = closed-loop stimulation; DDI = dual-chamber inhibited mode; RDR = rate-drop response; PM = pacemaker; pt = patients; N = number; FU = follow-up.

sec.title: Outcome
sec.p:
Overall, syncope recurrence occurred in 49 patients in the active pacing group, compared with 113 patients in the control group. Dual-chamber pacemaker implantation programmed either in rate-drop response or closed-loop stimulation mode or DDI with lower rate set at 80 bpm was associated with a 53% relative risk reduction in recurrent syncope (RR, 0.47 [95% CI, 0.27–0.82], p < 0.01; Fig. 2) All trials showed a favorable trend toward reduced syncope recurrence in the active pacing group, with the exception of a single study. Visual inspection of the funnel plot did not suggest marked asymmetry (Fig. 7). However, given the small number of included trials (n = 6), formal assessment of publication bias is underpowered, and funnel plot interpretation should be approached with caution. When the analysis was stratified by blinding status, dual-chamber pacing showed a non-significant trend toward reduced recurrence in blinded randomized trials (RR 0.59, 95% CI 0.32–1.08; p = 0.08; whereas a larger and statistically significant effect was observed in open-label trials (RR 0.22, 95% CI 0.11–0.46; p < 0.001; Fig. 3, Fig. 4). The difference in treatment effect between blinded and unblinded trials was statistically significant (p for interaction = 0.04), indicating that trial blinding modifies the observed efficacy of pacing. When the analysis was stratified by selection for documented asystolic vasovagal syncope, patients with asystolic episodes experienced a significant reduction in recurrent syncope with pacing (RR 0.35, 95% CI 0.22–0.55; p < 0.01; Fig. 5). In contrast, in patients not specifically selected for asystolic episodes, the effect was less pronounced and not statistically significant (RR 0.53, 95% CI 0.23–1.22; p = 0.12; Fig. 6). The test for interaction between these subgroups did not reach statistical significance (p for interaction = 0.39), indicating that although the within-subgroup effect in patients with documented asystole was statistically significant, formal evidence of effect modification was not demonstrated. Therefore, subgroup differences should be interpreted cautiously. Sensitivity analysis using restricted maximum likelihood (REML) produced comparable results, with the pooled effect remaining statistically significant and 95% confidence intervals entirely below 1. These findings indicate that the observed treatment effect was robust to the choice of between-study variance estimator (Supplementary Fig. 2). To further explore sources of heterogeneity and study-level influence, a Baujat plot was generated (Supplementary Fig. 3). VPS II and SYNPACE contributed most to between-study heterogeneity, with VPS II also exerting the greatest influence on the pooled effect estimate. However, no individual trial appeared to disproportionately drive the overall result. These findings were concordant with the leave-one-out sensitivity analysis, confirming the robustness and stability of the pooled treatment effect.Fig. 2
Forest Plot – Overall Effect of Active Pacing. Effect of Dual-Chamber Pacing on Syncope Recurrence. Forest plot displaying the risk ratios (RR) for syncope recurrence in patients receiving active dual-chamber pacing (CLS, RDR, or DDI) versus control groups. Pooled analysis was performed using a random-effects model and demonstrates a significant reduction in syncope recurrence in the active pacing group. Each study is represented with point estimates and 95% confidence intervals. Abbreviations: CLS = closed-loop stimulation; DDI = dual-chamber inhibited mode; RDR = rate-drop response; RR = risk ratio; CI = confidence interval.Fig. 3
Forest Plot – Blinded Trials. Syncope Recurrence in Blinded Randomized Trials. Forest plot showing pooled risk ratios for syncope recurrence in blinded RCTs comparing active pacing (CLS, RDR, DDI) versus control. Pooled estimates were calculated using a random-effects model. The effect of pacing was attenuated compared with open-label studies and did not reach statistical significance, highlighting potential placebo/nocebo influence. Abbreviations: CLS = closed-loop stimulation; DDI = dual-chamber inhibited mode; RDR = rate-drop response; RR = risk ratio; CI = confidence interval; RCT = randomized controlled trial.Fig. 4
Forest Plot – Open-Label Trials. Syncope Recurrence in Open-Label Randomized Trials. Forest plot showing the effect of active dual-chamber pacing on syncope recurrence in open-label RCTs. Pooled analysis was performed using a fixed-effects model. Pacing demonstrated a statistically significant reduction in recurrent syncope, with a larger treatment effect compared with blinded studies, suggesting an influence of trial design on observed outcomes. Abbreviations: CLS = closed-loop stimulation; DDI = dual-chamber inhibited mode; RDR = rate-drop response; RR = risk ratio; CI = confidence interval; RCT = randomized controlled trial.Fig. 5
Forest Plot – Trials with Documented Asystolic VVS. Effect of Pacing in Patients with Documented Asystolic Episodes. Forest plot displaying risk ratios for syncope recurrence in patients with documented asystolic vasovagal syncope (ILR or tilt-induced asystole ≥ 3 s with syncope or ≥ 6 s without syncope). Pooled estimates were derived using a fixed-effects model. Active pacing significantly reduced syncope recurrence compared with control, confirming the importance of patient selection based on objective asystolic events. Abbreviations: CLS = closed-loop stimulation; DDI = dual-chamber inhibited mode; RDR = rate-drop response; RR = risk ratio; CI = confidence interval; ILR = implantable loop recorder; VVS = vasovagal syncope.Fig. 6
Forest Plot – Trials without Requirement for Documented Asystolic VVS. Effect of Pacing in Patients Not Selected for Asystolic Episodes. Forest plot showing risk ratios for syncope recurrence in patients not specifically selected for documented asystolic events. Pooled analysis was conducted using a random-effects model. The effect of active pacing was smaller and did not reach statistical significance, highlighting that benefit is greatest in patients with confirmed cardioinhibitory pauses. Abbreviations: CLS = closed-loop stimulation; DDI = dual-chamber inhibited mode; RDR = rate-drop response; RR = risk ratio; CI = confidence interval; VVS = vasovagal syncope.Fig. 7
Funnel Plot. Assessment of Publication Bias in Included Randomized Trials. Funnel plot depicting the distribution of studies included in the meta-analysis according to the standard error of the log risk ratio for syncope recurrence versus the effect estimate. Visual inspection indicates no marked asymmetry, suggesting a low likelihood of publication bias among the six randomized controlled trials evaluating dual-chamber pacing (CLS, RDR, or DDI) in recurrent cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope. Each point represents an individual study, with the vertical line indicating the pooled effect estimate. Abbreviations: CLS = closed-loop stimulation; DDI = dual-chamber inhibited mode; RDR = rate-drop response; RR = risk ratio; CI = confidence interval.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Randomized trials evaluating dual-chamber pacing for vasovagal syncope have historically produced conflicting results, largely due to differences in patient selection and study design. Early studies such as VPS I suggested a marked reduction in syncope recurrence with pacing [8], but the open-label design limited the interpretability of these findings, as placebo effects could not be excluded in a condition characterized by a subjective clinical endpoint. Subsequent blinded trials, including VPS II and SYNPACE, failed to demonstrate a significant benefit of pacing [9], [10], underscoring the critical role of blinding in syncope trials.

The present meta-analysis confirms that trial blinding substantially modifies the observed treatment effect. Open-label trials showed a large and statistically significant reduction in recurrent syncope [8], [17], whereas blinded randomized trials demonstrated only a non-significant trend toward benefit. This divergence strongly suggests that expectation and placebo effects may inflate the apparent efficacy of pacing in unblinded settings, reinforcing the importance of rigorous trial design when evaluating interventions for reflex syncope. Importantly, study-level diagnostics using the Baujat plot demonstrated that blinded trials clustered among those contributing most to between-study heterogeneity while simultaneously attenuating the pooled effect size. This pattern indicates that heterogeneity is largely methodologically structured rather than driven by statistical outliers, reinforcing the role of trial design as a key determinant of observed pacing efficacy. The recently published COMFORTS-II trial evaluating elastic compression stockings in recurrent VVS further underscores the susceptibility of reflex syncope trials to expectation and placebo effects, reinforcing the necessity of rigorous blinding in intervention studies. [11] Beyond blinding, appropriate patient selection emerges as the key determinant of pacing efficacy. Trials enrolling unselected patients with vasovagal syncope—many of whom likely had a predominant vasodepressive component—reported limited or absent benefit. In contrast, studies restricting enrollment to patients with objectively documented cardioinhibitory reflex syncope, particularly those with implantable loop recorder–documented or tilt-induced asystole, consistently demonstrated a clinically meaningful reduction in syncope recurrence [12]. ISSUE-3 and BioSync CLS exemplify this paradigm, providing robust evidence that pacing is most effective in patients with a pure or predominant cardioinhibitory phenotype [6], [13]. In this meta-analysis, although patients with documented asystolic reflex syncope showed a marked reduction in recurrence with pacing, the formal test for interaction between asystolic and non-asystolic subgroups was not statistically significant. Consequently, while the observed benefit is biologically plausible and clinically compelling, the data do not definitively establish asystolic selection as a statistically confirmed effect modifier. These findings should therefore be interpreted as supportive rather than conclusive evidence.

Technological advances in pacing algorithms may further influence outcomes. Closed-loop stimulation (CLS), which dynamically adapts pacing based on beat-to-beat changes in intracardiac impedance, may allow earlier intervention during the vasovagal reflex compared with conventional rate-drop response algorithms. Randomized trials employing CLS have shown greater reductions in syncope recurrence, supporting the concept that anticipatory pacing may more effectively counteract the cardioinhibitory component of vasovagal syncope [14], [15], [16]. Pooling heterogeneous pacing strategies (CLS, RDR, DDI) may have diluted modality-specific effects, particularly given the distinct physiological mechanisms underlying closed-loop stimulation compared with conventional rate-drop response algorithms. The small number of trials employing CLS or reporting ILR-documented spontaneous asystole limited the feasibility of formal subgroup analyses. Therefore, pooled effect estimates integrate heterogeneous pacing strategies and diagnostic criteria, potentially attenuating modality-specific effects. Any apparent subgroup differences should be regarded as hypothesis-generating rather than confirmatory.

Overall, the findings of this meta-analysis reconcile previously conflicting trial results by demonstrating that both trial blinding and patient selection critically influence observed outcomes. Dual-chamber pacing should therefore not be considered a universal therapy for vasovagal syncope, but rather a targeted intervention for carefully selected patients with documented cardioinhibitory pauses. This interpretation closely aligns with current ESC guideline recommendations and supports a mechanism-based approach to the management of recurrent vasovagal syncope.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This meta-analysis has several limitations. First, there was substantial heterogeneity among the included randomized trials, both in terms of inclusion criteria and study arms. Second, the pacing modalities in the experimental arms were not uniform, encompassing both closed-loop stimulation (CLS) and rate-drop response (RDR) programming, which may have influenced treatment effects. Only one trial used CLS and one reported ILR-documented asystole; thus, subgroup analyses were not feasible, and pooled estimates should be interpreted as reflecting a mix of pacing modalities and asystole definitions. Third, Control-arm heterogeneity represents a major methodological limitation. Trials without pacemaker implantation in the control group inherently precluded blinding and may have amplified treatment effects through expectation mechanisms. These differences in trial design, intervention, and control modalities likely contributed to heterogeneity and may have affected the generalizability of the findings. Additionally, the small number of included trials limited the ability to reliably assess publication bias. Funnel plot analysis in meta-analyses including fewer than 10 studies has low sensitivity and should be interpreted cautiously.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Across randomized trials, dual-chamber pacing was associated with a lower incidence of syncope recurrence in patients with recurrent vasovagal syncope of likely cardioinhibitory origin. However, this effect was largely observed in open-label studies, whereas blinded trials did not demonstrate a statistically significant benefit, emphasizing the importance of trial design when interpreting the efficacy of pacing in this setting.

sec.title: Clinical competencies
sec.p: Medical Knowledge: Dual-chamber pacing, particularly with closed-loop stimulation, significantly reduces recurrence of cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope in patients with documented asystolic events. Awareness of the impact of patient selection and trial design is crucial for interpreting pacing efficacy.

Patient Care: Clinicians should consider pacemaker implantation in patients ≥ 40 years with recurrent, unpredictable syncope and evidence of cardioinhibitory pauses, as objective documentation of asystole predicts the greatest benefit.

Systems-Based Practice: Implementation of implantable loop recorders or tilt-testing to identify cardioinhibitory phenotypes can optimize resource utilization and prevent unnecessary pacing in patients unlikely to benefit.

Practice-Based Learning and Improvement: Integration of meta-analytic evidence into clinical decision-making supports evidence-based recommendations for pacing in selected patients, guiding individualized therapy.

sec.title: Translational outlook
sec.p: Future research should refine patient selection criteria, compare pacing modalities, and explore mechanistic predictors of response to dual-chamber pacing. Prospective trials with standardized protocols may further clarify the optimal strategy to prevent recurrent syncope and improve quality of life.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Francesco Sbarra: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Gianluca Pagnoni: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Susan Darroudi: Writing – review & editing. Luca Moscetti: Writing – review & editing. Aurora Vicenzi: Writing – review & editing. Maria G. Bolognesi: Writing – review & editing. Francesca Coppi: Writing – review & editing. Luca Rossi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Daniela Aschieri: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: None.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

element-citation.article-title: Lifetime cumulative incidence of syncope in the general population: a study of 549 dutch subjects aged 35-60 years
2021 ESC guidelines on cardiac pacing and cardiac resynchronization therapy
Assessment of a standardized algorithm for cardiac pacing in older patients affected by severe unpredictable reflex syncopes
2017 ACC/AHA/HRS guideline for the evaluation and management of patients with syncope
Cardiac pacing in severe recurrent reflex syncope and tilt-induced asystole
PRISMA Group. Preferred reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analyses: the PRISMA statement
The north American Vasovagal Pacemaker Study (VPS): a randomized trial of permanent cardiac pacing for the prevention of vasovagal syncope
Pacemaker therapy for prevention of syncope in patients with recurrent severe vasovagal syncope: Second Vasovagal Pacemaker Study (VPS II)
A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study of permanent pacing for the treatment of recurrent tilt-induced vasovagal syncope: the SYNPACE study
Using compression stockings to prevent recurrence of vasovagal syncope: a randomized sham-controlled trial
Early application of an implantable loop recorder allows mechanism-based effective therapy in patients with recurrent suspected neurally mediated syncope
International Study on Syncope of Uncertain Etiology 3 (ISSUE-3) investigators. Pacemaker therapy in patients with neurally mediated syncope and documented asystole
The effect of dual-chamber closed-loop stimulation on syncope recurrence in patients with tilt-induced cardioinhibitory vasovagal syncope: a prospective, randomized, single-blind, crossover study
Closed-loop stimulation in prevention of vasovagal syncope: the Inotropy Controlled Pacing in Vasovagal Syncope (INVASY) study
Dual-chamber pacing with closed-loop stimulation in recurrent reflex vasovagal syncope: the SPAIN study
Dual-chamber pacing in the treatment of neurally mediated tilt-positive cardioinhibitory syncope: pacemaker versus no therapy

sec.label: Appendix A
sec.title: Supplementary data
sec.p: The following are the Supplementary data to this article:Supplementary Fig. 1

fn.label: Appendix A
fn.p: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijcha.2026.101903.
